title: US-Iran agreement isn't yet a game-changer for the global outlook
gist: Oxford Economics argues the US-Iran agreement reduces the tail risk of a recession-inducing oil price spike but does not yet accelerate the resumption of Strait of Hormuz oil shipping versus its June baseline, which already assumed shipping resumes in late July. The development reinforces its view that the Fed and BoE won't hike rates.

themes.theme: US-Iran deal and oil tail risk

insights.claim: The US-Iran agreement reduces the tail risk that dwindling oil inventories trigger a recession-inducing oil price spike, even though detailed terms are lacking.
insights.speaker: Anusha Suthenthiran
insights.so_what: Lower probability of an oil-driven recession scenario supports risk assets and removes a key downside catalyst for cyclical positioning.
insights.quote: the fact that both sides announced an agreement reduces the tail risk of dwindling oil inventories prompting a recession-inducing oil price spike.
insights.locator: The agreement between the US and Iran is a significant step

insights.claim: Oxford Economics' June baseline already assumed Strait of Hormuz shipping resumes in late July, so the agreement does not imply a faster oil-flow ramp-up over coming months.
insights.speaker: Anusha Suthenthiran
insights.so_what: The deal is already priced into the baseline; do not expect upside revisions to oil supply timing from the headline.
insights.quote: We already assumed shipping through the Strait would resume in late July.
insights.locator: The latest developments don't automatically point to a faster ramp-up

insights.claim: It will take time for Strait of Hormuz shipping to approach pre-war levels, with bumps expected before any full-blown deal.
insights.speaker: Anusha Suthenthiran
insights.so_what: A gradual, non-linear normalization keeps a geopolitical risk premium on energy and shipping into the second half.
insights.locator: But there will likely be bumps in the road

insights.claim: The deal will reduce headline inflation but provide only a limited economic boost, with 2026 calendar-year growth likely to fall outside the 2.8%-3.0% range of recent years.
insights.speaker: Anusha Suthenthiran
insights.so_what: Disinflation without a strong growth rebound keeps a stagflation-lite backdrop in play for asset allocation.
insights.quote: calendar year growth for 2026 is still likely to fall outside the 2.8% to 3.0% range of the past few years.
insights.locator: While this will reduce headline inflation

insights.claim: The agreement reinforces Oxford Economics' view that the Federal Reserve and Bank of England won't hike rates and lessens the chance that central banks that already raised rates hike again.
insights.speaker: Anusha Suthenthiran
insights.so_what: A capped policy-rate path for the Fed and BoE supports duration and rate-sensitive equities.
insights.quote: this reinforces our long-held view that the Federal Reserve and the Bank of England won't hike rates
insights.locator: Overall, this reinforces our long-held view
